approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check all possible bucket assignments by iterating through all combinations of server allocations. This approach exhaustively evaluates every distribution to find the optimal load balance.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Load Balancing
ladders.desc: Find optimal load distribution across servers by checking all possible bucket assignments.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the load balancing problem by exhaustively evaluating every possible way to assign hash buckets to servers. The intuition is that by checking all combinations, we guarantee finding the optimal solution, though at high computational cost. The algorithm works by iterating through all possible server assignments for each bucket, calculating the load on each server for each assignment, and tracking the minimum maximum load (makespan). For n buckets and k servers, there are k^n possible assignments. We generate these assignments using nested loops or recursion. For each assignment, we compute server loads by summing bucket sizes assigned to each server, find the maximum load, and update the minimum makespan. Time complexity is O(k^n * n) in worst case, but with fixed k=2 servers (common in this problem), it becomes O(2^n * n) ≈ O(n * 2^n). Space complexity is O(k) for storing current loads. This connects to L1 by using the assignment evaluation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include: not resetting loads between assignments, incorrect boundary conditions, and exponential blowup with large n. Real pattern: initialize min_max_load = INF; for each assignment: reset loads; assign buckets; compute max_load; update min_max_load.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Evaluate Assignment Makespan
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum server load for a given bucket assignment.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine calculates the makespan (maximum load) for a specific assignment of buckets to servers. The intuition is that to compare assignments, we need to compute their quality metric—the highest load on any server, which we want to minimize. The algorithm takes an assignment array (where assignment[i] = server index for bucket i) and bucket sizes, then computes loads per server by iterating through buckets and adding each bucket's size to its assigned server's load. Finally, it returns the maximum load across all servers. Time complexity is O(n + k) where n is buckets and k is servers—O(n) to accumulate loads, O(k) to find max. Space complexity is O(k) for the loads array. This feeds into L0 by being called for each assignment to evaluate its quality. Common mistakes: using wrong array indices, not initializing loads to zero, or incorrect max calculation. Real pattern: loads = [0]*k; for i in range(n): loads[assignment[i]] += buckets[i]; return max(loads).

examples.trace: Initialize loads S0=0, S1=0. Bucket 0 (size 1) to server 0: S0=1. Bucket 1 (size 2) to server 0: S0=3. Bucket 2 (size 3) to server 1: S1=3. Loads: [3, 3]. Max = 3.
Loads start [0,0]. Bucket 0 (5) to S0: [5,0]. Bucket 1 (10) to S1: [5,10]. Bucket 2 (5) to S0: [10,10]. Max = 10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Assignments
ladders.desc: Systematically produce all possible server assignments for buckets.
ladders.explanation: The core logic generates all k^n assignments of n buckets to k servers, representing each assignment as an array of length n with values 0 to k-1. The intuition is that to brute force, we must enumerate every combination—like counting in base-k from 0 to k^n-1. For k=2, this is all binary strings of length n. The algorithm uses recursion or iterative counting: recursively assign each bucket to each possible server, building assignments depth-first; or iteratively increment an integer and convert to base-k. Time complexity is O(k^n) to generate all assignments, with O(n) space for the current assignment array. This feeds into L1 by providing assignments to evaluate. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in base conversion, not handling k>2 correctly, or exponential explosion causing timeouts. Real pattern (recursive): def generate(idx, n, k, curr, result): if idx==n: result.append(curr.copy()); return; for s in range(k): curr[idx]=s; generate(idx+1, n, k, curr, result).

examples.trace: Start with empty assignment. Level 0: assign bucket 0 to server 0, recurse; at level 1: assign bucket 1 to server 0 → [0,0]; then to server 1 → [0,1]. Backtrack; assign bucket 0 to server 1, recurse; at level 1: assign bucket 1 to server 0 → [1,0]; then to server 1 → [1,1]. Total 4 assignments.
Single bucket: assign to server 0 → [0]; server 1 → [1]; server 2 → [2]. Total 3 assignments.

testCases.expected: 16 assignments (list omitted for brevity, count verified)

ladders.title: Building Block — Sum Array Elements by Group
ladders.desc: Accumulate values into groups based on indices.
ladders.explanation: This building block sums elements of an array into groups specified by an assignment array. The intuition is that load balancing requires aggregating bucket sizes per server—like a group-by sum operation. The algorithm takes a values array (bucket sizes) and an assignment array (same length), initializes an array of sums for each group (server), then iterates through each element, adding its value to the sum of its assigned group. Time complexity is O(n + k) where n is elements and k is groups—O(n) for accumulation, O(k) to initialize sums. Space complexity is O(k) for the sums array. This feeds into L2 by being part of assignment evaluation. Common mistakes: index out of bounds if assignment values exceed k-1, not zero-initializing sums, or misordering operations. Real pattern: sums = [0]*k; for i in range(len(values)): sums[assignment[i]] += values[i]; return sums.

examples.trace: Initialize sums [0,0]. i=0: value=1, assign=0 → sums[0]=1. i=1: value=2, assign=0 → sums[0]=3. i=2: value=3, assign=1 → sums[1]=3. Result [3,3].
Sums start [0,0]. i=0: 5 to group 0 → [5,0]. i=1: 10 to group 1 → [5,10]. i=2: 5 to group 0 → [10,10]. Result [10,10].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate and Accumulate
ladders.desc: Loop through an array and sum elements conditionally.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves iterating over an array and accumulating values based on simple conditions—fundamental to computing loads. The intuition is that many algorithms require processing each element once to compute aggregates. The algorithm initializes a sum variable, loops through each element, and adds its value to the sum. For conditional accumulation, include an if-statement to check a condition (e.g., group membership). Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, space O(1) for the sum variable. This feeds into L3 as the core of group summation. Common mistakes: off-by-one in loop bounds, not initializing sum to zero, or incorrect accumulation logic. Real pattern: total = 0; for val in array: total += val; return total. For conditional: sum = 0; for i in range(n): if condition(i): sum += array[i]; return sum.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. i=0: val=1, total=1. i=1: val=2, total=3. i=2: val=3, total=6. Return 6.
sum=0. i=0 (even): val=5, sum=5. i=1 (odd): skip. i=2 (even): val=5, sum=10. Return 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Loops
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers arrays (lists) and for-loops—prerequisites for any algorithm. An array is a collection of elements accessed by index, starting at 0. A loop repeats a block of code for each element. The intuition is that algorithms process data sequentially, and arrays with loops provide the basic structure. For an array of size n, indices go from 0 to n-1. A for-loop iterates through each index: for i in range(n): access array[i]. Time complexity for traversing an array is O(n), space O(1) if not storing extra data. This feeds into L4 as the mechanism for accumulation. Common mistakes: accessing index n (out of bounds), misunderstanding zero-based indexing, or infinite loops. Real pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]). Practice: create arrays, access elements, modify values in loops.

examples.trace: Initialize i=0: arr[0]=10, print 10. i=1: arr[1]=20, print 20. i=2: arr[2]=30, print 30. Loop ends.

approaches.name: Hash Modulo Distribution O(n)
approaches.summary: Use hash modulo operation to assign requests to servers based on request ID. This leverages the uniform distribution property of good hash functions to achieve approximate load balancing.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(k) where k is number of servers

ladders.title: Full Problem — Load Balancing by Hash Modulo Distribution
ladders.desc: Complete solution to assign requests to servers using hash modulo operation for approximate load balancing.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires distributing n requests across k servers to achieve approximate load balancing. The core intuition is that good hash functions produce uniformly distributed outputs, so applying modulo k to these hash values will distribute requests approximately evenly across servers. The algorithm works by: 1) For each request ID, compute a hash value using a reliable hash function. 2) Apply modulo k to map the hash value to a server index (0 to k-1). 3) Count how many requests map to each server. The time complexity is O(n) since we process each request once. Space complexity is O(k) for storing counts per server. This approach provides approximate rather than perfect balancing because hash collisions can occur, but with good hash functions and sufficient requests, the distribution tends toward uniformity. Common mistakes include: using poor hash functions that cluster values, forgetting to handle negative hash values (always take absolute value or use unsigned operations), and misunderstanding that this gives statistical rather than guaranteed perfect balancing. The connection to real systems is that this is exactly how many load balancers work in practice—simple, fast, and good enough for many applications.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize server_counts = [0, 0, 0]
Step 2: Process request 1001: hash(1001) = 1001 (simple hash for demo), 1001 % 3 = 2, increment server_counts[2] → [0, 0, 1]
Step 3: Process request 1002: hash(1002) = 1002, 1002 % 3 = 0, increment server_counts[0] → [1, 0, 1]
Step 4: Process request 1003: hash(1003) = 1003, 1003 % 3 = 1, increment server_counts[1] → [1, 1, 1]
Step 5: Process request 1004: hash(1004) = 1004, 1004 % 3 = 2, increment server_counts[2] → [1, 1, 2]
Step 6: Process request 1005: hash(1005) = 1005, 1005 % 3 = 0, increment server_counts[0] → [2, 1, 2]
Final: server_counts = [2, 1, 2]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Hash Modulo Mapping Function
ladders.desc: Function that takes a request ID and number of servers, returns which server should handle it.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the load balancing algorithm. The intuition is that we need a deterministic way to map any request to exactly one server, and hash modulo provides this while maintaining statistical balance. The algorithm: 1) Compute hash of the request ID using a standard hash function (like Python's built-in hash() or a custom consistent hash). 2) Take the absolute value to ensure non-negative (since modulo with negative numbers can be tricky). 3) Apply modulo with the number of servers to get a server index between 0 and k-1. Time complexity is O(1) per request. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full problem by being called for each request to determine its server assignment. Common mistakes: not handling hash collisions properly (different requests might hash to same value—this is okay as long as distribution is uniform), using modulo with zero servers (must handle this edge case), and forgetting that hash functions might return different values across program runs (use deterministic hashes). The real pattern is simple: server_index = abs(hash(request_id)) % k.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute hash(12345). Using Python's hash: hash(12345) = 12345 (simplified for demo).
Step 2: Take absolute value: abs(12345) = 12345.
Step 3: Apply modulo 5: 12345 % 5 = 0 (since 12345 ÷ 5 = 2469 remainder 0). Wait, let's recalculate: 12345 ÷ 5 = 2469 exactly, so remainder 0. Actually, 12345 % 5 = 0. But to show a non-zero example, let's use request_id = 12346: hash(12346)=12346, abs=12346, 12346 % 5 = 1 (since 12346 ÷ 5 = 2469 remainder 1). So output would be 1. For original 12345, output is 0.

Step 1: hash(999) = 999 (simplified).
Step 2: abs(999) = 999.
Step 3: 999 % 7 = 5 (since 999 ÷ 7 = 142 remainder 5).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Uniform Distribution via Modulo Operation
ladders.desc: Understanding how modulo operation on hash values creates approximately even distribution.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that when you take uniformly distributed numbers (from a good hash function) and apply modulo k, the results will be approximately uniformly distributed across 0 to k-1. Intuition: Imagine hash values as random points on a number line. When you divide by k and take remainder, you're effectively wrapping the number line around a circle of circumference k. If the original points are uniformly spread, the remainders will also be spread evenly around the circle. Algorithmically: given a set of hash values H, for each h in H, compute h % k. Statistically, as size of H increases, the counts for each remainder value approach |H|/k. Time and space are O(1) conceptually for understanding the distribution property. This feeds into the key sub-routine by justifying why hash modulo works for load balancing. Common mistakes: assuming perfect uniformity (it's approximate), not realizing that poor hash functions ruin the distribution, and misunderstanding that modulo works on the hash value not the original request ID directly (which might not be uniformly distributed). Real pattern: visualize hash values mod k creating buckets.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute each modulo:
10 % 3 = 1
20 % 3 = 2
30 % 3 = 0
40 % 3 = 1
50 % 3 = 2
Step 2: Count frequencies:
Remainder 0: count 1 (from 30)
Remainder 1: count 2 (from 10, 40)
Remainder 2: count 2 (from 20, 50)
Distribution is approximately even (1,2,2) around average 1.67.

Step 1: Compute modulo:
5 % 4 = 1
15 % 4 = 3
25 % 4 = 1
35 % 4 = 3
45 % 4 = 1
55 % 4 = 3
Step 2: Count:
0: 0
1: 3 (from 5,25,45)
2: 0
3: 3 (from 15,35,55)
Not perfectly uniform due to specific values, but with more random hashes it improves.

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Function Basics
ladders.desc: Understanding what hash functions are and their properties relevant to load balancing.
ladders.explanation: A hash function takes an input (like a request ID) and returns a fixed-size value (hash) such that: 1) Different inputs ideally produce different hashes (minimize collisions). 2) The output is uniformly distributed across the possible hash space. 3) It's deterministic (same input always gives same hash). For load balancing, we need a hash function that spreads requests evenly. Intuition: think of a hash function as a blender—it takes structured input and produces a seemingly random number. The algorithm for a simple hash (for illustration): initialize hash to a seed, for each character in input, combine with hash using arithmetic operations. Time O(L) where L is input length, space O(1). This feeds into core logic by providing the uniformly distributed numbers needed for modulo operation. Common mistakes: using weak hash functions that create patterns (like identity hash h(x)=x, which fails if request IDs are sequential), not handling different data types properly, and assuming cryptographic hashes are needed (they're slower; non-cryptographic hashes like MurmurHash are fine). Real pattern: use built-in language hash functions or implement a simple one like polynomial rolling hash.

examples.trace:
Step 1: For integer, simplest hash is identity: hash(x) = x.
Step 2: So hash(4567) = 4567.
Note: This is a poor hash for load balancing if request IDs are sequential, but works for demo.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of computing remainder after division.
ladders.explanation: Modulo operation, denoted as a % b, returns the remainder when a is divided by b. Intuition: if you have a number and you want to wrap it around a fixed range (0 to b-1), modulo does exactly that. For example, hours on a clock use modulo 12. Algorithm: given integers a and b (b > 0), compute quotient q = floor(a / b), then remainder r = a - q*b. Time O(1), space O(1). This feeds into hash function usage because after hashing, we use modulo to map the large hash value to a server index. Common mistakes: misunderstanding negative numbers (in programming, a % b can be negative if a is negative; we usually take absolute value first), dividing by zero (must check b != 0), and confusing modulo with integer division. Real pattern: server_index = hash_value % num_servers, ensuring hash_value is non-negative.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Divide 17 by 5: 17 ÷ 5 = 3 remainder 2.
Step 2: So 17 % 5 = 2.
Check: 3*5 = 15, 17 - 15 = 2.

Step 1: 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1.
Step 2: 10 % 3 = 1.
Check: 3*3=9, 10-9=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of arrays and how to access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index (usually starting at 0). Intuition: think of a row of lockers numbered 0, 1, 2, etc.; you can put something in locker i and later retrieve it by knowing i. Algorithm: to create an array of size k for server counts, initialize all elements to 0. To increment the count for server i, access array[i] and add 1. Time O(1) for access/update, space O(k) for storage. This feeds into basic operations because after computing server index via modulo, we use that index to update the corresponding array element. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index k when array size is k, valid indices are 0 to k-1), forgetting to initialize array elements, and confusing zero-based with one-based indexing. Real pattern: counts = [0] * k; then counts[server_index] += 1.

examples.input: Create array of size 3, increment index 1, then index 0
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize array = [0, 0, 0].
Step 2: Increment at index 1: array[1] was 0, becomes 1 → [0, 1, 0].
Step 3: Increment at index 0: array[0] was 0, becomes 1 → [1, 1, 0].

examples.input: Array size 5, increment index 2 twice, then index 4 once
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize [0,0,0,0,0].
Step 2: Increment index 2: [0,0,1,0,0].
Step 3: Increment index 2 again: [0,0,2,0,0].
Step 4: Increment index 4: [0,0,2,0,1].

testCases.input: size = 1, increments at index 0 three times

approaches.name: Consistent Hashing O(log k)
approaches.summary: Map both servers and requests onto a hash ring, assigning each request to the nearest server clockwise. This minimizes redistribution when servers are added or removed from the system.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log k) per request where k is number of servers

ladders.title: Full Problem — Load Balancing with Consistent Hashing
ladders.desc: Implement consistent hashing to assign requests to servers while minimizing redistribution when servers are added or removed.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing consistent hashing to solve load balancing. The intuition is to map both servers and requests onto a virtual hash ring (circle) using a hash function, then assign each request to the nearest server clockwise. This approach minimizes redistribution when servers are added or removed because only requests near the affected server's position on the ring need to be reassigned, unlike traditional modulo-based hashing which redistributes most requests. The algorithm involves: 1) Creating a sorted data structure (like a balanced BST or sorted list) to store server positions on the ring. 2) Hashing each server to a position on the ring (e.g., using SHA-1 and modulo 360 for degrees). 3) For each request, hash it to a position, then find the nearest server clockwise using binary search (O(log k) time). 4) Handling server additions by inserting the new server's position and reassigning only requests between its predecessor and itself. 5) Handling server removals by removing its position and reassigning its requests to the next server clockwise. Time complexity is O(log k) per request for lookup, O(log k) for server updates, and O(k) space for storing server positions. Connection to L1: This builds on the key subroutine of finding the nearest server. Common mistakes include not handling hash collisions (use unique IDs or virtual nodes), incorrect binary search implementation for circular ring (wrap-around), and not updating requests after server changes. Real code pattern: Use sortedcontainers.SortedList in Python for O(log n) operations.

examples.output: Initial assignments: r1->s1, r2->s2, r3->s3, r4->s1; After adding s4: r1->s1, r2->s2, r3->s3, r4->s4; After removing s2: r1->s1, r2->s3, r3->s3, r4->s4
examples.trace: Step 1: Hash servers to ring positions (simplified: s1=30°, s2=150°, s3=270°). Step 2: Hash requests (r1=20°, r2=100°, r3=200°, r4=350°). Step 3: Assign requests: r1 at 20° -> nearest clockwise server is s1 at 30°; r2 at 100° -> s2 at 150°; r3 at 200° -> s3 at 270°; r4 at 350° -> wrap to s1 at 30°. Step 4: Add s4 at 200°: only r3 at 200° reassigns from s3 to s4. Step 5: Remove s2 at 150°: r2 at 100° reassigns to s3 at 270° (next clockwise).

examples.output: Initial: x->a, y->b, z->a; After add c: x->a, y->b, z->c; After remove a: x->b, y->b, z->c
examples.trace: Step 1: Hash servers: a=50°, b=200°. Step 2: Hash requests: x=40°, y=150°, z=300°. Step 3: Assign: x at 40° -> a at 50°; y at 150° -> b at 200°; z at 300° -> wrap to a at 50°. Step 4: Add c at 100°: y at 150° reassigns from b to c? Wait, y at 150° is between c at 100° and b at 200°, so nearest clockwise is still b at 200° (since 150° to 200° is 50°, while 150° to 100° would be 310° clockwise). Actually, for clockwise: from 150°, c at 100° is 310° away (360-150+100=310), b at 200° is 50° away, so b is closer. No reassignment. z at 300°: nearest clockwise is a at 50° (110° away) vs c at 100° (160° away) vs b at 200° (260° away), so still a. Wait, correction: when adding c at 100°, requests between its predecessor and itself reassign. Predecessor of c is a at 50°, so requests from 50° to 100° reassign to c. But our requests: x at 40° (not between), y at 150° (not between), z at 300° (not between). So no reassignment. Output should be: initial: x->a, y->b, z->a; after add c: same; after remove a: x at 40° reassigns to b at 200° (160° away) or c at 100° (60° away)? Clockwise from 40°: c at 100° is 60°, b at 200° is 160°, so c is closer. Thus x->c, y->b, z->c. Let's recalc: After remove a, servers: b=200°, c=100°. Requests: x=40° -> clockwise to c at 100° (60°); y=150° -> b at 200° (50°); z=300° -> wrap to c at 100° (160° vs b at 200° 260°, so c). So final: x->c, y->b, z->c.

testCases.input: servers: ["s1"], requests: ["r1"], add_server: null, remove_server: null
servers: ["s1", "s2"], requests: ["r1", "r2"], add_server: null, remove_server: null
servers: ["s1", "s2"], requests: ["r1", "r2", "r3"], add_server: null, remove_server: null

testCases.expected: initial: x->a; after add d: x->a
initial: x->a, y->b; after add c: x->a, y->b; after remove a: x->c, y->b
initial: r1->s1, r2->s2, r3->s3, r4->s1; after add s4: r1->s1, r2->s2, r3->s4, r4->s1; after remove s2: r1->s1, r2->s3, r3->s4, r4->s1
initial: no assignment; after add s1: r1->s1
initial: r1->s1; after remove s1: no assignment

testCases.input: servers: ["x", "y", "z"], requests: ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e"], add_server: null, remove_server: null

testCases.expected: initial: r1->s1; after add s3: r1->s1; after remove s1: r1->s2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Nearest Server Clockwise on Hash Ring
ladders.desc: Given a sorted list of server positions on a circle and a request position, find the server with the smallest clockwise distance.
ladders.explanation: This is the core operation in consistent hashing: for each request, we need to efficiently find the nearest server moving clockwise on the hash ring. The intuition is to treat the ring as a circle with positions from 0 to 360 (or 0 to 2^32), and use binary search to find the first server position greater than or equal to the request position. If no such server exists (i.e., request position is greater than all servers), we wrap around to the first server on the ring. This achieves O(log k) time complexity. The algorithm: 1) Sort server positions (done once during initialization). 2) For a request position r, perform binary search on the sorted list to find the leftmost server position >= r. 3) If found, return that server. 4) If not found (r > all servers), return the first server in the list (wrap-around). Time complexity is O(log k) per request, with O(k) space for storing positions. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for every request assignment and server update. Common mistakes include not handling wrap-around correctly, off-by-one errors in binary search, and assuming positions are unique (use tie-breaking). Real code pattern: Use bisect_left in Python for binary search.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sorted servers: [30, 150, 270]. Step 2: Request r=100. Step 3: Binary search for first server >=100: 30<100, 150>=100, so index 1. Step 4: Return server at position 150.
Step 1: Servers: [50, 200]. Step 2: r=300. Step 3: Binary search: 50<300, 200<300, no server >=300. Step 4: Wrap-around: return first server, position 50.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Mapping to a Hash Ring for Minimal Redistribution
ladders.desc: Understand how placing servers and requests on a circle reduces reassignments during changes.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of consistent hashing is that by mapping servers and requests onto a circle (hash ring), we minimize the number of reassignments when servers are added or removed. Traditional hashing (e.g., modulo-based) redistributes almost all requests when the number of servers changes, causing massive overhead. In contrast, on a hash ring, each request is assigned to the nearest server clockwise. When a server is added, only requests that hash between its predecessor and itself on the ring need to be reassigned to the new server. When a server is removed, only its requests are reassigned to the next server clockwise. This limits redistribution to approximately n/k requests on average, where n is requests and k is servers. The intuition: the circle creates a continuous space where each server 'owns' the arc from its predecessor to itself. Changes affect only the boundaries of these arcs. This is why consistent hashing is widely used in distributed systems like Dynamo and Cassandra. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by the find-nearest-server subroutine. Common mistakes include not using a good hash function (should be uniform), not handling virtual nodes for better load distribution, and not considering the ring as circular. Real code pattern: Use a hash function like SHA-1 modulo ring size.

examples.input: Servers at positions 0, 120, 240 on a 360-degree ring. Add server at position 60.
examples.output: Only requests between 0 and 60 reassign to new server.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initially, server 0 owns arc 240-0, server 120 owns 0-120, server 240 owns 120-240. Step 2: Add server at 60. Step 3: New server's predecessor is 0, so it owns arc 0-60. Step 4: Requests in arc 0-60 reassign from server 120 to new server. Other arcs unchanged.

examples.output: Requests in arc 180-90 reassign to server 180.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initially, server 90 owns arc 180-90, server 180 owns 90-180. Step 2: Remove server 90. Step 3: Its requests (arc 180-90) reassign to next clockwise server: 180. Step 4: Server 180 now owns arc 180-180 (full circle).

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to find an element or insertion point in a sorted list.
ladders.explanation: Binary search is a fundamental algorithm used to efficiently find an element in a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition is that because the array is sorted, we can compare the target with the middle element and eliminate half of the remaining elements each step, achieving O(log n) time complexity. For consistent hashing, we use a variant to find the first element greater than or equal to a target (bisect_left). The algorithm: 1) Initialize low=0, high=n-1. 2) While low <= high: compute mid = (low+high)//2. 3) If array[mid] == target, return mid (or adjust for first occurrence). 4) If array[mid] < target, set low = mid+1. 5) Else, set high = mid-1. 6) After loop, low is the insertion point for target. Time complexity O(log n), space O(1). Connection to L2: This enables O(log k) lookup on the hash ring. Common mistakes include integer overflow in mid calculation (use low + (high-low)//2), off-by-one errors, and not handling empty arrays. Real code pattern: Standard binary search loop.

examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=2, array[2]=30 == target, return 2.
Step 1: low=0, high=2, mid=1, array[1]=15 <20, low=2. Step 2: low=2, high=2, mid=2, array[2]=25 >=20, high=1. Step 3: low=2 > high=1, exit. Return low=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Hashing to a Numerical Value
ladders.desc: Convert a string (server or request ID) to a number using a hash function.
ladders.explanation: Hashing is the process of taking an input (like a string) and mapping it to a fixed-size numerical value, called a hash code. In consistent hashing, we hash server and request identifiers to positions on the ring (e.g., an angle from 0 to 360). The intuition is that a good hash function distributes inputs uniformly across the output range, ensuring load balance. Properties needed: deterministic (same input always gives same output), uniform distribution, and fast computation. Common hash functions include MD5, SHA-1, or simpler ones like polynomial rolling hash. For consistent hashing, we often use cryptographic hashes for uniformity. The algorithm: 1) Choose a hash function (e.g., SHA-1). 2) Apply it to the input string to get a byte array. 3) Convert bytes to an integer. 4) Take modulo ring size (e.g., 360) to get position. Time complexity O(1) per hash, space O(1). Connection to L3: This provides the positions for binary search. Common mistakes include using weak hash functions (causing collisions), not modulo properly, and not handling negative values. Real code pattern: Use hashlib.sha1 in Python.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute SHA-1 hash of "server1" -> byte string. Step 2: Convert to integer (e.g., 123456). Step 3: Modulo 360: 123456 % 360 = 150. Step 4: Position is 150 degrees.
Step 1: Hash "requestA" -> integer. Step 2: % 100 -> 75.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Modular Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Learn how modulo operation works and its use in wrapping values.
ladders.explanation: Modular arithmetic is the arithmetic of remainders, fundamental to hashing and circular structures. The modulo operation (a % b) gives the remainder when a is divided by b. For example, 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 divided by 3 is 2 with remainder 1. In consistent hashing, we use modulo to map hash values to a fixed range (e.g., 0 to 359 for a 360-degree ring), creating a circular wrap-around. The intuition: when we reach the end of the range, we wrap back to the start, like a clock. This enables the hash ring concept. Key properties: (a % b) is always between 0 and b-1 inclusive. Negative numbers: in programming, (-1) % 360 might be 359 (Python) or -1 (C++), so handle carefully. Connection to L4: Modulo is used after hashing to get ring position. Common mistakes include confusion with integer division, off-by-one errors, and not understanding negative cases. Real code pattern: Use % operator directly.

examples.trace: Step 1: 10 divided by 3 is 3 with remainder 1. Step 2: 10 % 3 = 1.
Step 1: 365 divided by 360 is 1 with remainder 5. Step 2: 365 % 360 = 5.
